advantages: Broad AI definition includes rules, search, and learning—matching decades of research and diverse production techniques.
Clarifies that ML is a powerful subset of AI, not the whole field, preventing category errors when discussing non-learning systems.
Narrow AI framing sets realistic expectations: task-specific systems we can measure, deploy, and improve today.
Separating narrow AI from AGI avoids marketing hype that treats chatbots as human-level general intelligence.
disadvantages: Broad definition is too wide for procurement or regulation—'AI-powered' can mean anything from a spreadsheet macro to a billion-parameter model.
Narrow colloquial usage excludes legitimate AI (planners, expert systems) and over-centers on statistical learning.
Dual vocabulary confuses stakeholders: engineers say 'ML pipeline' while executives say 'AI strategy' for the same work.
AGI discourse distracts from concrete risks and quality bars of narrow systems that actually ship.
